results.question: I have a thick perfume oil, like honey consistency. What roll-on bottles do you recommend?
results.response:
I want to make sure you get the best result here — thick, honey-consistency oils are actually a poor match for roll-on applicators. The roller ball relies on free-spinning contact with the liquid to dispense, and with a viscous oil, it simply won't spin freely. You'd end up with clogging, uneven application, and frustrated customers.

For a thick perfume oil like an attar or absolute, the two applicators I'd point you toward are:

A tola bottle — the traditional vessel for attar and concentrated perfume oil. It's designed for direct dabbing application and is the industry standard for thick, luxurious oils. Elegant, tactile, and completely appropriate for the viscosity you're describing.

A dropper — a glass or plastic dropper gives you precise, controlled dispensing of thick oil without any mechanical parts to clog. It's also very popular in the wellness and essential oil space, and lends a clinical-luxe aesthetic.

Would you like me to pull up our tola and dropper options so you can see what's available?

results.question: I'm making an eau de parfum. Should I use roll-on bottles?
results.response:
Great question — and an important one to get right before ordering.

Eau de parfum is alcohol-based and quite thin in viscosity. Roll-on applicators are engineered for oils and serums — the roller ball relies on a certain viscosity to spin freely and dispense properly. With a thin, alcohol-based EDP, you'd likely experience leaking around the ball and inconsistent application.

For an EDP, a fine mist sprayer is the natural choice. It delivers that signature soft, diffuse spray that fine fragrance is designed for — and it's what your customers will expect from a premium scent experience.

What bottle style are you drawn to — something sleek and architectural, something more classic and rounded, or perhaps something with a bit of decorative presence?

results.question: I'm packaging a thick body lotion. Would a dropper bottle work?
results.response:
For a thick body lotion, a dropper wouldn't be the right fit — the viscosity is too heavy for a dropper to dispense smoothly, and it can clog or fail to draw up properly.

What you'd want is a lotion pump, which is purpose-built for thick emulsions. It delivers a controlled, mess-free dose every time and gives the product a polished, professional feel on shelf.

Would you like me to pull up our lotion pump bottle options, or do you have a size and aesthetic in mind already?

results.question: Tell me about your Boston Round collection.
results.response:
The Boston Round is one of our most versatile vessels — a classic apothecary silhouette with gently sloping shoulders, available in three sizes: 15ml, 30ml, and 60ml. The collection comes in clear, amber, and cobalt blue glass, with amber being especially popular for essential oils and light-sensitive formulas.

For application, the family supports droppers, metal roller balls, and plastic roller balls, making it a natural fit for essential oils, serums, beard oils, and aromatherapy blends. The neck finishes are 18-400 on the smaller size and 20-400 on the larger two, and the entire collection spans over 120 variants.

results.question: Do you manufacture your own glass?
results.response:
We don't manufacture glass — Best Bottles is a sourcing and supply partner, not a producer. We curate our collection through a global network of artisan glassmakers, bringing together vessels that meet our standards for finish quality, consistency, and compatibility.

What sets us apart is that we actually use these same bottles for Nemat's own fragrance lines, sold at Sephora, Ulta, and Whole Foods — so every piece in our catalog is validated in the real world, not just on a spec sheet.

Are you exploring packaging for a specific product or brand?

results.question: Why should I choose Best Bottles over other suppliers?
results.response:
That's a fair question, and worth answering directly.

Most packaging suppliers fall into one of two camps: industrial distributors who treat fragrance the same as shampoo, or generic wholesale sites with no guidance and no accountability. Best Bottles occupies a different space entirely.

Here's what sets us apart in practice:

The practitioner advantage. We're the packaging division of Nemat International — a family fragrance house whose products are on shelves at Sephora, Ulta, and Whole Foods. We don't just sell these bottles; we use them for our own retail line. That means every vessel in our collection has been validated through real-world production, not just a spec sheet.

The system guarantee. Components sold together through Best Bottles are guaranteed to fit. The number-one pain point in this industry is incompatible components — a cap that almost seats, a sprayer that wobbles, a roller ball that leaks. We source complete systems and test thread tolerances so you don't discover a mismatch after a 500-unit run.

The scaling gap. We serve brands in the $50K–$5M range who've outgrown DIY suppliers but aren't large enough for enterprise distributors. No unit minimums, no account requirements, no gatekeeping. You can start with a small test order and scale into production runs — same products, same quality, same partner.

The expertise. Our concierge service exists to prevent costly mistakes: wrong applicator for your formula's viscosity, mismatched neck finishes, decorating techniques that won't hold. That guidance is built in.

What stage is your brand at right now — are you prototyping, or looking at a production run?

results.question: I'm launching a luxury perfume line. Should I go with Elegant or Diva bottles?
results.response:
Here's how they compare:

The Elegant comes in four sizes — 15ml, 30ml, 60ml, and 100ml — in both clear and frosted glass, with the full range of applicators including fine mist sprayer, vintage bulb sprayer, roll-on, dropper, and reducer. It's the more versatile canvas, and that slender form reads beautifully with frosted glass and a gold or silver trim.

The Diva offers three sizes — 30ml, 46ml, and 100ml — also in clear and frosted, with spray, dropper, lotion pump, and vintage bulb sprayer options. Note that the Diva does not come with a roll-on applicator. Its curvature is genuinely distinctive on shelf — it photographs exceptionally well and has a collectible quality.

One meaningful differentiator: the Diva's sizes are consistent across a single 18-415 neck finish, which simplifies sourcing if you want interchangeable closures across your line. The Elegant spans multiple neck sizes depending on the variant.

What aesthetic direction is your brand taking — classic and understated, or bold and sculptural?

results.question: I need bottles for my essential oil business. Where do I start?
results.response:
Essential oils are a wonderful category to work with — and getting the packaging right makes a real difference in how your product performs and how your brand is perceived.

A few quick questions to point you in the right direction:

Are your oils light and free-flowing, or more on the thick and viscous side? And are you thinking about a dropper for precise application, a roll-on for direct skin use, or something else entirely?

results.question: What does 18-415 mean? I see it on your product pages.
results.response:
Great question. The notation "18-415" is a neck finish specification — it tells you two things about a bottle's opening:

The first number, 18, is the outer diameter of the bottle neck in millimetres. The second number, 415, refers to the thread pattern — specifically the pitch and style of the threads that allow a closure to grip and seal.

So when you see 18-415 on a bottle and 18-415 on a cap or sprayer, you know they'll thread together correctly. It's the industry-standard shorthand used across US cosmetic and fragrance packaging.

Would you like me to look up which closures and applicators are compatible with the 18-415 finish?
